narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 36 and an undergraduate GPA of 2.8, you've worked hard to build a strong profile for law school, including your middle family income background and plans to attend full-time like most students. Unfortunately, the admissions model predicted a 60% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is the threshold that led to your application's rejection—schools use this to prioritize candidates likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the typical student's 5th or 6th decile, signaling to the model that your later academic performance raised concerns about bar exam readiness. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also lower than average, added to this worry by reinforcing a pattern of underperformance relative to peers. On a brighter note, your LSAT score, close to the dataset average of 36.7, strongly pushed the model toward expecting you to pass, acting as your most significant positive influence. Your undergraduate GPA, though slightly below the 3.21 average, provided a modest boost in the same direction, while your full-time status aligned with the vast majority of successful students and helped a bit too. Family income in the middle bracket had only a minor nudge toward the failure prediction compared to others. Overall, your profile stands out for lower decile rankings in school grades versus typical admits who rank higher, despite your competitive LSAT keeping you in the conversation. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background—many in similar situations improve with targeted effort. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger coursework or prep programs, retake the LSAT if possible to exceed 37, and consider part-time options or gap-year experience to demonstrate growth. Reapply after addressing these gaps, perhaps with letters highlighting your resilience, and explore schools with holistic reviews that value your full story. You've got the foundation—now refine it to shift that prediction in your favor. We're rooting for you.
